title: Minimum cut in an undirected graph (Stoer–Wagner) (advanced).
description: Minimum cut in an undirected graph (Stoer–Wagner) (advanced).

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices: edges (0,1) weight 2, (1,2) weight 3, (0,2) weight 1

approaches.name: Brute Force O(2^n * n^2)
approaches.summary: Enumerate all possible partitions of vertices into two sets and compute the cut value for each by summing edge weights across the partition. Use this for small graphs to understand the problem, but it's impractical for larger graphs due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Minimum Cut in Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Find the minimum cut value by enumerating all possible vertex partitions and computing cut weights.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the minimum cut problem by systematically checking every possible way to split the graph's vertices into two non-empty subsets (source side S and sink side T). For each partition, we calculate the cut value by summing weights of all edges crossing between S and T. The minimum across all partitions is the answer. Intuition: Since a cut is defined by which vertices are on each side, and there are 2^n possible assignments (each vertex can be in S or T), we can exhaustively search this space. However, we exclude trivial partitions where S or T is empty (since cut value would be 0), and we treat (S,T) and (T,S) as the same cut. The algorithm generates all subsets of vertices (excluding empty and full sets), computes the cut for each by iterating through edges and adding weights when endpoints are on opposite sides, and tracks the minimum. Time complexity is O(2^n * n^2): 2^n subsets, and for each, we check up to n^2 edges (in dense graphs). Space is O(n^2) to store the adjacency matrix. This approach is foundational for understanding the problem but impractical beyond tiny graphs (n ≤ 15-20). Connection to real-world: Useful for small network partitioning problems. Common mistakes: Forgetting to exclude empty/full subsets, double-counting symmetric cuts, or inefficient edge iteration. Real code pattern uses bitmasking for subset generation and adjacency matrix for quick weight lookup.

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices: edges (0,1) weight 2, (1,2) weight 3, (0,2) weight 1

testCases.input: 3 vertices: triangle with all edges weight 1
4 vertices: complete graph with all edges weight 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Cut Value for a Given Partition
ladders.desc: Calculate the total weight of edges crossing between two vertex subsets.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine takes a partition of vertices into sets S and T (with S ∪ T = all vertices, S ∩ T = ∅) and computes the cut value: sum of weights of edges with one endpoint in S and the other in T. Intuition: A cut separates the graph into two parts; edges crossing this separation contribute to the cut's cost. The algorithm iterates through all edges (or all pairs of vertices in dense representation). For each edge (u,v) with weight w, if u and v are in different sets (one in S, one in T), add w to a running total. Efficient implementation uses an adjacency matrix for O(1) weight lookup and O(n^2) time per partition. Alternatively, adjacency lists reduce to O(m) where m is edges. Time complexity is O(n^2) for dense graphs, O(m) for sparse. Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This is called for each of the 2^n subsets in the brute force solution. Common mistakes: Double-counting edges if iterating unordered pairs incorrectly, missing edges in sparse representation, or using slow weight lookups. Real code pattern uses nested loops over vertices and checks membership via bitmask or set operations.

examples.trace: Edge (0,1): both in S, ignore. Edge (0,2): 0 in S, 2 in T, add 3 → total=3. Edge (1,3): 1 in S, 3 in T, add 1 → total=4. No other edges. Final cut value=4.

examples.input: Graph: vertices {0,1,2}, edges: (0,1)=5, (1,2)=6. Partition: S={0}, T={1,2}
examples.trace: Edge (0,1): 0 in S, 1 in T, add 5 → total=5. Edge (1,2): both in T, ignore. Final cut value=5.

testCases.input: Graph: 4 vertices, complete graph weight 1. S={0,1}, T={2,3}
Graph: 4 vertices, no edges. S={0,1}, T={2,3}
Graph: 5 vertices, star centered at 0 with weights 2. S={0}, T={1,2,3,4}
Graph: 3 vertices, triangle weights 2. S={0}, T={1,2}
Graph: 5 vertices, single edge (2,4)=9. S={2}, T={0,1,3,4}
Graph: 5 vertices, clique weight 3. S={0,1}, T={2,3,4}

ladders.title: Core Logic — Enumerate All Non-Empty Proper Subsets of Vertices
ladders.desc: Generate all possible ways to split vertices into two non-empty groups for cut evaluation.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves generating all subsets of the vertex set that are neither empty nor the full set (since those correspond to trivial cuts with value 0). For n vertices, there are 2^n total subsets, but we exclude the empty set and the full set, leaving 2^n - 2 subsets. However, each cut (S,T) is symmetric with (T,S), so we typically generate only subsets up to size ⌊n/2⌋ to avoid duplicates, but in brute force we might generate all and divide by 2. Intuition: Every cut is uniquely defined by which vertices are on one side (e.g., source side S), as the other side T is the complement. By iterating over all possible S (non-empty and not all vertices), we cover all cuts. Algorithm: Use bitmasking from 1 to (1<<n)-2 (excluding 0 and (1<<n)-1). For each mask, S = vertices where bit is 1, T = complement. Time O(2^n) to generate, space O(n) to store current subset. Connection to L1: Each subset is passed to the cut value computation. Common mistakes: Including empty/full subsets, double-counting symmetric cuts inefficiently, or using recursive generation that is too slow. Real code pattern uses a for loop with integer mask and extracts vertices via bit operations.

examples.trace: Bitmask from 1 to (1<<3)-2 = 1 to 6. Mask 1 (001): S={0}. Mask 2 (010): S={1}. Mask 3 (011): S={0,1}. Mask 4 (100): S={2}. Mask 5 (101): S={0,2}. Mask 6 (110): S={1,2}. Exclude mask 0 (empty) and 7 (full).
Bitmask from 1 to (1<<2)-2 = 1 to 2. Mask 1 (01): S={0}. Mask 2 (10): S={1}. Note: {0} and {1} are symmetric cuts.

ladders.title: Building Block — Represent Graph as Adjacency Matrix for Fast Weight Lookup
ladders.desc: Store edge weights in a 2D array for constant-time access during cut computation.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency matrix is a square matrix where entry matrix[i][j] stores the weight of edge between vertex i and vertex j (or 0 if no edge). For undirected graphs, it's symmetric: matrix[i][j] = matrix[j][i]. Intuition: This data structure allows O(1) time to check if an edge exists and get its weight, which is crucial when computing cut values repeatedly in brute force. Construction: Given n vertices and a list of edges with weights, initialize an n x n matrix with zeros. For each edge (u,v,w), set matrix[u][v] = matrix[v][u] = w. Time O(n^2) to initialize and O(m) to fill, space O(n^2). Connection to L2: Used in subset enumeration to quickly sum weights across cuts. Common mistakes: Not handling missing edges (should be 0), forgetting symmetry for undirected graphs, or using inefficient sparse structures when dense is needed. Real code pattern uses a list of lists in Python, with careful indexing.

examples.trace: Create 3x3 zero matrix. Edge (0,1)=2: set matrix[0][1]=2, matrix[1][0]=2. Edge (1,2)=3: set matrix[1][2]=3, matrix[2][1]=3. All other entries remain 0.
Create 4x4 zero matrix. For all i≠j, set matrix[i][j]=1. Diagonal stays 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over All Pairs of Vertices to Sum Edge Weights
ladders.desc: Loop through each unordered pair (i,j) to accumulate weights based on partition membership.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves scanning all possible vertex pairs (i,j) where i < j to avoid double-counting edges in an undirected graph. For each pair, check if an edge exists (weight > 0) and if vertices i and j are in different sets of the partition. If so, add the weight to a running total. Intuition: Since edges are unordered, we only need to consider each pair once. In dense graphs, this is O(n^2) per partition. Algorithm: Initialize sum=0. For i from 0 to n-1, for j from i+1 to n-1, if weight[i][j] > 0 and ((i in S and j in T) or (i in T and j in S)), then sum += weight[i][j]. Time O(n^2), space O(1). Connection to L3: Uses the adjacency matrix to get weights quickly. Common mistakes: Double-counting by using i,j and j,i, missing edges with zero weight, or inefficient membership checks. Real code pattern uses nested loops and bitmask checks.

examples.trace: Pairs: (0,1): weight=2, 0 in S, 1 in T → add 2, sum=2. (0,2): weight=0, ignore. (1,2): weight=3, both in T → ignore. Final sum=5.
Pairs: (0,1): weight=1, both in S → ignore. (0,2): weight=1, 0 in S, 2 in T → add 1, sum=1. (1,2): weight=1, 1 in S, 2 in T → add 1, sum=2. Final sum=2.

testCases.input: Matrix: 4x4 with 1 on all off-diagonals, S={0,1}, T={2,3}
Matrix: 5x5 with weight 2 on (1,3) only, S={1}, T={0,2,3,4}
Matrix: 4x4 identity pattern (not symmetric), but treat as undirected with given weights, S={0,2}, T={1,3}

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Graph Cuts and Edge Weights
ladders.desc: Learn what a cut is in an undirected graph and how to sum weights of crossing edges.
ladders.explanation: A cut in an undirected graph is a partition of the vertices into two disjoint, non-empty subsets, often called S (source side) and T (sink side). The cut set is the set of edges that have one endpoint in S and the other in T. The cut value (or weight) is the sum of weights of edges in the cut set. Intuition: Imagine cutting the graph into two pieces by removing edges; the total weight of removed edges is the cut value. In unweighted graphs, weights are 1, so cut value is the number of crossing edges. Example: For a graph with vertices {A,B,C} and edges A-B (weight 2), B-C (weight 3), if S={A}, T={B,C}, the cut edges are A-B, so cut value=2. Step-by-step: 1. Define vertex sets S and T. 2. List all edges. 3. For each edge, check if its endpoints are in different sets. 4. If yes, add its weight to total. Time O(m) to check edges, space O(1). Connection to L4: This is the fundamental operation scaled up. Common mistakes: Confusing directed/undirected cuts, including edges within S or T, or misunderstanding weight summation. Real code pattern uses simple conditionals.

examples.input: Graph: vertices {X,Y,Z}, edges: X-Y=4, Y-Z=5. Cut: S={X,Y}, T={Z}
examples.trace: Edge X-Y: both in S, ignore. Edge Y-Z: Y in S, Z in T, add 5. Total cut value=5.

examples.input: Graph: vertices {P,Q,R,S}, edges: P-Q=1, Q-R=2, R-S=3, S-P=4. Cut: S={P,R}, T={Q,S}
examples.trace: Edge P-Q: P in S, Q in T, add 1 → sum=1. Q-R: Q in T, R in S, add 2 → sum=3. R-S: R in S, S in T, add 3 → sum=6. S-P: S in T, P in S, add 4 → sum=10.

testCases.input: Vertices {A,B}, edge A-B=7. Cut S={A}, T={B}
Vertices {A,B,C}, edges A-B=2, B-C=3. Cut S={A,C}, T={B}
Vertices {a,b,c}, no edges. Cut S={a}, T={b,c}
Vertices {x,y,z}, edges x-y=10, y-z=20, z-x=30. Cut S={x}, T={y,z}
Vertices {p,q,r}, edges p-q=3. Cut S={p,r}, T={q}
Vertices {a,b,c,d}, edges a-b=2, c-d=4. Cut S={a,c}, T={b,d}
Vertices {1,2}, edge 1-2=0 (zero weight). Cut S={1}, T={2}
Vertices {u,v,w}, edges u-v=1, v-w=1, w-u=1. Cut S={u}, T={v,w}

approaches.name: Karger's Randomized Algorithm O(n^2 log n)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly contract random edges until only two vertices remain, representing a cut, and repeat the process multiple times to find the minimum cut with high probability. This is a Monte Carlo algorithm suitable for undirected graphs with uniform edge weights.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Cut in Undirected Graph using Karger's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement Karger's randomized algorithm to find the minimum cut in an undirected graph with uniform edge weights.
ladders.explanation: Karger's algorithm is a Monte Carlo algorithm that finds the minimum cut in an undirected graph by repeatedly contracting random edges until only two vertices remain, representing a cut. The algorithm runs multiple iterations to achieve a high probability of finding the minimum cut. The intuition is that by randomly contracting edges, we reduce the graph while preserving the minimum cut with reasonable probability. The algorithm works because each contraction reduces the number of vertices by one, and the probability of not contracting an edge from the minimum cut is at least 1/2 in each contraction. By repeating the process O(n² log n) times, the probability of success becomes high. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize the graph with n vertices and m edges. 2. While there are more than 2 vertices: a. Pick a random edge uniformly. b. Contract the two endpoints of the edge into a single vertex, merging their adjacency lists and removing self-loops. 3. The remaining edges between the two vertices represent a cut. 4. Repeat steps 1-3 multiple times (typically O(n² log n) times) and keep the smallest cut found. Time complexity is O(n² log n) per iteration due to edge contractions and adjacency list updates, with O(n²) space for storing the graph. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key subroutine of random edge contraction repeatedly. Common mistakes include not removing self-loops correctly during contraction, which can lead to incorrect cut sizes, and insufficient iterations to guarantee high probability. Real code pattern involves maintaining adjacency lists and using union-find or direct merging for contractions.

examples.trace: Iteration 1: Start with vertices {0,1,2,3}. Random edge (1,2) chosen. Contract 1 and 2 into supervertex A. Graph now: vertices {A,0,3}, edges: (A,0) from (0,1) and (0,2), (A,3) from (1,3) and (2,3), self-loop (A,A) from (1,2) removed. Random edge (A,0) chosen. Contract A and 0 into supervertex B. Graph now: vertices {B,3}, edges: (B,3) from (A,3). Cut size = 1. Iteration 2: Start fresh. Random edge (0,1) chosen. Contract 0 and 1 into supervertex C. Graph: vertices {C,2,3}, edges: (C,2) from (0,2) and (1,2), (C,3) from (1,3), (2,3). Random edge (2,3) chosen. Contract 2 and 3 into supervertex D. Graph: vertices {C,D}, edges: (C,D) from (C,2) and (C,3) and (2,3)? Wait, need to merge carefully: After first contraction, edges: (C,2), (C,3), (2,3). Contract 2 and 3 into D: edges become (C,D) with weight 2? Let's trace: Original edges: (0,1), (0,2), (1,2), (1,3), (2,3). Contract (0,1) -> C. Merged edges: (C,2) from (0,2) and (1,2) -> count 2, (C,3) from (1,3) -> count 1, (2,3) remains. Now vertices: C,2,3. Contract (2,3) -> D. Edges: (C,D) from (C,2) and (C,3) -> total 2+1=3? But (2,3) was between 2 and 3, so it becomes self-loop on D and is removed. So cut size = 3. Minimum over iterations is 1, but actual min cut is 2 (cut separating {0,3} from {1,2}). Need more iterations. After many iterations, smallest found is 2.

Triangle graph. Iteration 1: Random edge (0,1) chosen. Contract to supervertex A. Graph: vertices {A,2}, edges: (A,2) from (1,2) and (2,0) -> count 2. Cut size = 2. Iteration 2: Random edge (1,2) chosen. Contract to supervertex B. Graph: vertices {B,0}, edges: (B,0) from (0,1) and (2,0) -> count 2. Cut size = 2. Iteration 3: Random edge (2,0) chosen. Contract to supervertex C. Graph: vertices {C,1}, edges: (C,1) from (0,1) and (1,2) -> count 2. All cuts are 2, so min cut = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Random Edge Contraction in a Graph
ladders.desc: Contract a randomly chosen edge in an undirected graph, merging its endpoints into a single vertex.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core operation in Karger's algorithm, where we repeatedly contract edges to reduce the graph size. The intuition is that by merging two vertices connected by an edge, we simulate the removal of that edge from consideration in the cut, effectively reducing the graph while preserving the structure of other cuts. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Represent the graph using adjacency lists or an edge list. 2. Select a random edge uniformly from all current edges. 3. Identify the two vertices u and v of the edge. 4. Merge v into u (or vice versa): a. Combine the adjacency lists of u and v. b. Remove any edges that become self-loops (i.e., edges between u and v or within the merged vertex). c. Update all references to v to point to u. 5. Decrease the vertex count by one. Time complexity is O(n) per contraction in adjacency list representation, as we need to traverse and update lists. Space complexity is O(n + m) for storing the graph. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main algorithm until only two vertices remain. Common mistakes include not correctly handling parallel edges or self-loops, which can lead to incorrect edge counts. For example, when merging, self-loops must be removed because they don't contribute to cuts between different vertices. Real code pattern involves using a union-find structure to efficiently track merged vertices or directly modifying adjacency lists.

examples.trace: Start: vertices: 0,1,2; edges: 0-1, 1-2. Choose edge (0,1). Merge vertex 1 into 0. Combine adjacency: 0's neighbors: [1], 1's neighbors: [0,2] -> new neighbors for 0: [1,2]. But 1 is now merged, so remove self-loop edge to 1. Update: edge 0-1 becomes self-loop, remove it. Edge 1-2 becomes edge 0-2. Final: vertices: 0,2; edges: 0-2.

examples.output: New graph: vertices {0,1}, edges: (0,1) with weight 2 (from original (0,1) and (0,2) after merging).
examples.trace: Start: vertices: 0,1,2; edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-2. Choose edge (1,2). Merge vertex 2 into 1. Combine adjacency: 1's neighbors: [0,2], 2's neighbors: [0,1] -> new for 1: [0,2,0,1]. Remove self-loops: edge to 2 is now self-loop (since 2 merged into 1), remove it. Edge to 0 appears twice? Actually, after merging, edges: 0-1 (original), 0-2 becomes 0-1, and 1-2 becomes self-loop removed. So we have two edges 0-1? Yes, parallel edges allowed. Final: vertices: 0,1; edges: 0-1 (count 2).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Probability of Preserving the Minimum Cut During Contractions
ladders.desc: Understand why randomly contracting edges has a reasonable chance of not contracting an edge from the minimum cut.
ladders.explanation: The central insight of Karger's algorithm is that the probability of successfully preserving the minimum cut throughout all contractions is at least 1/(n choose 2), which is sufficiently high when repeated many times. The intuition is that in each contraction step, we pick a random edge uniformly from all edges. The minimum cut has size k (the smallest number of edges crossing some partition). The total number of edges in the graph is at least (k * n)/2 by the handshaking lemma? Actually, more precisely: if the minimum cut size is k, then each vertex has degree at least k (since otherwise, that vertex would form a smaller cut). Thus, total edges m ≥ (k * n)/2. The probability of picking an edge from the minimum cut in a given contraction is at most k/m ≤ 2/n. Therefore, the probability of not picking such an edge is at least 1 - 2/n. Over n-2 contractions (to reduce from n vertices to 2), the probability of never contracting a minimum cut edge is at least ∏_{i=0}^{n-3} (1 - 2/(n-i)) = 2/(n(n-1)) = 1/(n choose 2). This is relatively small for large n, but by repeating the algorithm O(n² log n) times, the probability of missing the minimum cut becomes exponentially small. Step-by-step: 1. Start with n vertices. 2. In each contraction, compute the probability of avoiding min cut edges. 3. Multiply probabilities across all contractions. Time/space analysis: This is a probabilistic analysis, not an algorithm, so no computational complexity. Connection to L1: This logic justifies why the random contraction subroutine, when repeated, can find the min cut with high probability. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the degree bound or miscalculating the product. Real code pattern doesn't directly implement this but uses it to determine the number of iterations needed.

examples.input: Graph with n=4 vertices, minimum cut size k=2. Compute probability of preserving min cut in one contraction.
examples.trace: Given n=4, k=2. Minimum degree ≥ k=2, so total edges m ≥ (k*n)/2 = (2*4)/2 = 4. Probability of picking a min cut edge in one contraction ≤ k/m = 2/4 = 1/2. So probability of not picking it ≥ 1 - 1/2 = 1/2.

examples.input: Graph with n=3 vertices, minimum cut size k=2 (triangle graph). Compute overall probability of preserving min cut through all contractions.
examples.trace: n=3, k=2. Total edges m=3 (since triangle). Probability in first contraction: not picking min cut edge? Min cut edges are any 2 edges? Actually, in triangle, min cut size is 2, any partition of 1 vertex vs 2 gives cut size 2. So any edge could be in some min cut. But using formula: probability of not picking min cut edge in first contraction ≥ 1 - 2/m = 1 - 2/3 = 1/3. After first contraction, n becomes 2, so only one contraction needed? For n=3, we need n-2=1 contraction to reduce to 2 vertices. So overall probability ≥ 1/3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing an Undirected Graph with Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Create and manipulate an undirected graph data structure using adjacency lists, supporting edge queries and updates.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for implementing Karger's algorithm efficiently. An adjacency list representation stores each vertex along with a list of its neighboring vertices, which allows for quick traversal and modification during edge contractions. The intuition is that by maintaining dynamic lists of neighbors, we can easily merge vertices and update connections. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize a list or dictionary where each key is a vertex and the value is a list of adjacent vertices. 2. For each edge (u,v), add v to u's list and u to v's list to represent undirectedness. 3. To contract an edge (u,v): a. Append all neighbors of v to u's list. b. For each neighbor w of v, update w's list: replace v with u. c. Remove self-loops from u's list (i.e., occurrences of u or v). d. Remove vertex v from the graph. Time complexity for contraction is O(degree(v)) for updating lists, which is O(n) in worst case. Space complexity is O(n + m) for storing all lists. Connection to L2: This representation enables the random edge contraction by providing efficient access to edges. Common mistakes include not handling parallel edges correctly (they can be stored as multiple entries in the list) or forgetting to update both endpoints when modifying edges. Real code pattern in Python: use a dictionary of lists, e.g., graph = {0: [1,2], 1: [0,2], 2: [0,1]} for a triangle.

examples.input: Create graph for vertices 0,1,2 with edges (0,1) and (1,2).
examples.trace: Start with empty dict. Add edge (0,1): append 1 to list for 0, append 0 to list for 1. So graph becomes {0: [1], 1: [0]}. Add edge (1,2): append 2 to list for 1, append 1 to list for 2. Final: {0: [1], 1: [0,2], 2: [1]}.

Start: {0: [1], 1: [0,2], 2: [1]}. Contract edge (0,1): merge vertex 1 into 0. Combine lists: 0's list: [1] + 1's list [0,2] -> [1,0,2]. Remove self-loops: remove 1 (since merged) and 0 (self-loop) -> new list for 0: [2]. Update neighbor 2: its list [1] becomes [0] (replace 1 with 0). Remove vertex 1. Final: {0: [2], 2: [0]}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Selecting a Random Element from a List
ladders.desc: Choose a random element uniformly from a list, a fundamental operation for picking edges in Karger's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is used to randomly select an edge for contraction. The intuition is that by picking uniformly at random, each edge has an equal chance of being chosen, which is crucial for the probabilistic guarantees of the algorithm. Step-by-step: 1. Have a list of elements (e.g., edges represented as pairs of vertices). 2. Generate a random integer index between 0 and the length of the list minus 1. 3. Return the element at that index. Time complexity is O(1) for the selection, assuming random number generation is constant time. Space complexity is O(1) additional space. Connection to L3: This operation is used within the adjacency list representation to pick a random edge during contraction. Common mistakes include not ensuring uniformity (e.g., if the list changes during iteration) or using a non-random seed. Real code pattern in Python: use random.randint(0, len(list)-1) or random.choice(list).

examples.output: One of the three pairs, e.g., ('c','d').
examples.trace: List has 3 elements. Generate random index between 0 and 2. Suppose index 1 is chosen. Return element at index 1: ('c','d').
List length 5. Random index between 0 and 4. Suppose index 2. Return element at index 2: 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Graph Cut?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic definition of a cut in an undirected graph: a partition of vertices into two sets.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for understanding minimum cut problems. A cut in an undirected graph is defined as a partition of the vertex set into two disjoint subsets, say S and T. The cut itself refers to the set of edges that have one endpoint in S and the other in T. The size (or weight) of the cut is the number (or total weight) of these crossing edges. The intuition is that a cut represents a way to separate the graph into two parts, and the minimum cut is the partition that requires removing the fewest edges to disconnect the graph. Step-by-step: 1. Take a graph with vertices V. 2. Choose any non-empty proper subset S of V (i.e., S ≠ ∅ and S ≠ V). 3. Define T = V \ S. 4. The cut edges are all edges (u,v) such that u ∈ S and v ∈ T. 5. Count these edges to get the cut size. Time/space analysis: Conceptual, no computational cost. Connection to L4: This foundation is needed to understand why we care about edges when contracting randomly. Common mistakes include confusing cut with other graph concepts like paths or cycles, or not ensuring S and T are non-empty. Real code pattern doesn't directly implement cuts but uses them to evaluate results.

examples.input: Graph: vertices {0,1,2}, edges: (0,1), (1,2). Define cut S={0}, T={1,2}.
examples.trace: Vertices: 0,1,2. S = {0}, T = {1,2}. Edges: (0,1) has 0 in S and 1 in T, so it's in the cut. Edge (1,2) has both endpoints in T, so not in cut. Thus, cut edges: {(0,1)}. Size = 1.

examples.input: Graph: vertices {0,1,2}, edges: (0,1), (1,2), (2,0). Define cut S={0,1}, T={2}.
examples.trace: S = {0,1}, T = {2}. Edges: (0,1) both in S, not in cut. (1,2): 1 in S, 2 in T -> in cut. (2,0): 2 in T, 0 in S -> in cut. So cut edges: {(1,2), (2,0)}. Size = 2.
